data.title: Asia markets slump as Iran vows permanent Hormuz closure; oil spikes on day 14 of conflict
data.description:
Asian stocks traded lower on Friday after Wall Street’s overnight weakness as the U.S.–Iran conflict entered its 14th day. Investors reacted sharply to a pledge from Iran’s new Supreme Leader, Mojtaba Khamenei, to maintain a blockade of the Strait of Hormuz, coupled with intensified strikes on regional oil infrastructure.

The resulting surge in crude prices has reignited global inflation fears, WTI crude futures traded around $95 per barrel on Friday after a sharp two-day rally.

Furthermore, the Trump administration is weighing a temporary waiver of the Jones Act, the century-old law requiring U.S.-flagged ships to carry goods between domestic ports, as part of efforts to ease surging oil and gasoline prices. 

The commodities and digital asset desks saw a surge in defensive positioning with Bitcoin (BTC-USD) rose nearly 1% toward $71,200 in mid-February, extending its rally to a fifth consecutive session.

Gold prices rose to around $5,110 per ounce on Friday, following a two-day drop.

The benchmark KOSPI slipped 1.30% to around 5,510 on Friday, extending losses for another consecutive session. The South Korean won (USD:KRW) weakened just below 1,490 per dollar, extending losses near its weakest level since 2009.

Japan (NKY:IND) fell 1.42% to around 53,700, while the broader Topix Index lost 1% to 3,612 on Friday, sliding for the second straight session. The Japanese yen traded around 159.4 per dollar on Friday, hovering near its weakest levels since July 2024, raising concerns of possible intervention by authorities.

The Nikkei fell, impacted by a more than 6% drop in Honda Motor Co Ltd (HMC), which expects an annual loss from restructuring costs.

Japan 10 Year Government Bond Yield increased to 2.24%, the highest since February 2026.

China (SHCOMP) fell 0.13% near 4,129 on Friday and was poised to finish the week largely unchanged, and the offshore yuan depreciated to around 6.88 per dollar on Friday, extending losses from the previous session as investors weighed fresh U.S. trade probes targeting several economies, including China.

In China, equities edged lower after two sessions of gains as traders awaited activity data for January–February, notably industrial output, retail sales, and the surveyed jobless rate, due next week.

Hong Kong (HSI) fell 0.56% to 25,495 in Friday morning trade, marking a third straight decline.

India (SENSEX) fell 1.16% to below 75,416 on Friday, marking its third consecutive session of losses to its lowest levels since April 2025.

Foreign investors have also sold about $49 billion of Indian stocks in March, the biggest outflow since January 2025. India inflation also rose to an eleven-month high of 3.21% in February, up from 2.74% in January and above expectations of 3.1%, reflecting a return to more normal levels after last year’s unusually low food-driven readings. 

Australia (AS51) fell 0.14% to around 8,600 in Friday morning trade, extending losses from the previous session, and headed for its second weekly drop. The Australian dollar held its recent decline to around $0.709.

In the U.S. on Thursday, all three major indexes ended in red as markets feared that energy-fueled inflation will deter the Federal Reserve from cutting interest rates any time soon.

U.S. stock futures edged higher on Friday after heavy losses in the prior session, as investors prepared for another key inflation reading: Dow +0.32%; S&P 500 +0.19%; Nasdaq +0.05%.

Currencies: (JPY:USD), (CNY:USD), (AUD:USD), (INR:USD), (HKD:USD), (NZD:USD).

More on Asia's markets and economy:

data.title: Build-A-Bear signals at least 50 new global locations in 2026 while expanding international partner model
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Build-A-Bear Workshop, Inc. (BBW) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Sharon John, President, CEO & Director, announced her retirement effective June 11, 2026, with Chief Operations Officer Chris Hurt set to become CEO, highlighting "a multiyear planned succession process." John emphasized, "we ultimately delivered a solid year with both pretax income and revenue within our guidance range. In fact, we hit an important milestone for the company by delivering more than $0.5 billion in annual revenue for the first time in Build-A-Bear's history."
  • Chris Hurt, Chief Operations & Experience Officer, reported, "we made significant progress, adding 11 net new experience locations across 4 continents, bringing our total to 64. For the year, we entered 8 new countries, following the 10 countries added in 2024. This doubles our international footprint to 36 countries in just 2 years."
  • Hurt announced expansion plans: "we expect to open at least 50 net new locations in 2026, with the majority of those openings in our partner-operated asset-light model."
  • John described a digital setback: "most of our focus last year was on evolving behind-the-scenes infrastructure... some of our previously planned e-commerce advancements... were delayed, contributing to disappointing online sales."
  • John also noted a new IP initiative: "our KABU's character Plush has already surpassed $1 million in sales."
  • Vojin Todorovic, Chief Financial Officer, stated, "we met our guidance and delivered our fifth consecutive year of record results, underscoring the durability of our business model. We grew across all segments, expanded gross margin and increased pretax income versus last year, even with the absorption of a negative tariff impact."

Outlook

  • Todorovic stated, "we expect total revenues to grow at a mid-single-digit rate, driven in part by the addition of at least 50 net new experience locations, the majority of which are expected to be international partner-operated."
  • Revenue growth is expected to accelerate as the year progresses, with first quarter revenue "roughly flat with last year."
  • "Pre-tax income is expected to range from a mid-single-digit decline to low single-digit growth, reflecting a $16 million full estimated impact from tariffs and tariff-related costs and approximately $3 million in longer-range investments."
  • In the Commercial segment, "we expect revenue growth of at least 20% for the year with significant back half weighting."

Financial Results

  • Todorovic reported, "Total revenues for the quarter were $154.5 million, an increase of 2.7% year-over-year."
  • Net retail sales for the fourth quarter were $139.5 million.
  • E-commerce demand decreased 13.6% for the quarter, with full-year e-commerce down 5.5%.
  • Commercial revenue increased 42.2% for the quarter.
  • Gross margin for the quarter was 55.2%.
  • SG&A expense was $63.9 million or 41.4% of total revenues.
  • Pre-tax income was $21.5 million compared to $27.5 million last year.
  • Earnings per share was $1.26 compared to $1.62 last year.
  • Fiscal 2025 annual revenues were $529.8 million, up 6.7% year-over-year. EPS for the year was $3.99.

Q&A

  • Eric Beder, Small Cap Consumer Research, LLC, asked about in-store personalization and expansion. John responded, "We already have embroidery online, but having that kind of in-store visible experience for consumers is important... We see this as an opportunity to expand in key markets, more of these large tourist locations."
  • Beder also inquired about inventory flows. Todorovic explained, "our inventory, we finished the year a little bit more elevated compared to last year... we are making investments in inventory to support the growth and expansion."
  • Keegan Cox, D.A. Davidson & Co., asked about the $3 million in long-term investments. Todorovic stated, "ICON Park... these are long-term investments, and there is just like elevated preopening expenses... growth of wholesale, international expansion, we are making these investments upfront and revenue is expected to come at a little bit later date."
  • Cox also probed international expansion. Hurt replied, "last year, we did open in 8 countries... Italy is our biggest area where we have 15 partner-operated locations right now."
  • Christopher Moore, CJS Securities, Inc., asked about pretax margin guidance. Todorovic answered, "we have about -- just between those 2 things, about $8 million of additional costs."
  • Moore inquired about mix of global locations. Hurt said, "70% and 30% in our international business."
  • Steven Silver, Argus Research Company, asked about product sourcing and tariffs. Todorovic explained, "Inventory management and supply chain disruptions over the last 12, 18 months have been really challenging... we have executed well considering the uncertainty."
  • Gregory Gibas, Northland Capital Markets, asked about SEO headwinds. John stated, "that direct click-through through organic search has significantly declined... that's a double-digit impact to the direct click-through, which is very measurable."
  • Gibas followed up on Mini Beans trends. John replied, "we sold 3 million Mini Beans... we're still seeing positive trends on Mini Beans."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was primarily inquisitive and focused on understanding the impact of tariffs, inventory flows, international partner expansion, digital headwinds, and product trends. Questions were detailed and occasionally pressing, especially around cost and guidance.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, frequently expressing gratitude and emphasizing resilience and strategy. During Q&A, management responses were thorough, occasionally defensive regarding tariffs and inventory but generally optimistic, with phrases such as "we believe..." and "we continue to..." recurring.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst sentiment remained consistent, focused on operational risks and international expansion, while management's confidence in long-term strategy was stable, though more cautious on near-term digital and tariff challenges.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Current quarter guidance introduced a mid-single-digit revenue growth target and flagged incremental $16 million in tariffs and $3 million in long-term investments, compared to reaffirmed guidance and less pronounced tariff impact in Q3.
  • Strategic focus expanded to include a detailed four-pillar framework with heightened emphasis on international partner-operated growth, omnichannel optimization, and wholesale/licensing.
  • Analysts continued to focus on tariffs, digital transformation, and international expansion, with added attention to digital channel headwinds.
  • Management's tone was positive but more defensive on digital challenges and tariffs than the previous quarter, reflecting evolving uncertainties and disruptions.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted ongoing risks from "unexpected tariff and global supply chain disruptions."
  • E-commerce underperformance and digital traffic declines due to "aggressive rollout of AI-driven changes by Google" were noted as significant headwinds.
  • Tariff costs expected to increase, with management cautioning on "the amount and timing of any potential tariff changes or refunds remain uncertain."
  • Inventory levels elevated due to tariff inclusion and growth investments, but management stated, "we are diligent in managing our inventory and our expenses."
  • Analysts raised concerns about the sustainability of margin performance under these pressures and the cadence of international expansion.

Final Takeaway

Build-A-Bear Workshop delivered record annual revenues and expanded its global footprint to 36 countries, driven by a combination of new experience locations and the asset-light partner-operated model. Management outlined a robust strategic framework centered on organic growth, international expansion, wholesale/licensing, and gifting/personalization. While digital traffic and tariff-related headwinds are expected to persist, the company projects mid-single-digit revenue growth for 2026, with at least 50 net new locations planned—primarily internationally. Leadership transition to Chris Hurt as CEO is set for June 2026, with continued focus on scaling the brand and executing long-term growth initiatives despite ongoing operational and macroeconomic challenges.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Earnings Snapshot: Wheaton Precious Metals forecasts 2026 annual output of 860,000–940,000 GEOs after Q4 beat
Earnings Snapshot: SentinelOne Q1 guidance falls below estimates; FY profit outlook tops expectations
Trump administration takes aim at CNN over airing Iranian leaders - report
data.description:
The Trump administration criticized CNN for broadcasting part of a message from Iran’s newly appointed supreme leader, Mojtaba Khamenei, amid escalating tensions between the U.S. and Iran.

The White House accused CNN, "fake news CNN just aired four straight minutes of uninterrupted Iranian state TV, run by the same psychotic and murderous regime that prided itself on brutally slaughtering Americans for 47 years." This followed CNN showing a Farsi reading with an English translation of Khamenei's remarks, his first since succeeding his father, who was killed in an Israeli airstrike.

Other outlets like Sky News, Al Jazeera, AP, and The New York Times also covered it, highlighting vows of regional attacks and oil supply threats.

Two days back, White House communications director Steven Cheung also criticized the network anchor Erin Burnett's interview with former Iranian nuclear negotiator Seyed Hossein Mousavian. 

"Ever notice how CNN just regurgitates quotes and unverified information from Iranian terrorists? Total disgrace. They have become the murderous Iranian Regime’s version of Pravda." Cheung wrote on his social media and compared the network to the Soviet-era propaganda outlet Pravda.

CNN did not address Cheung's statement but did respond to the White House attack. The network defended airing the content for its "obvious news value" amid global war watching. “The world is watching with anticipation which direction this war will take,” CNN said.

data.title: ByteDance gains access to Nvidia’s top AI chips despite China export restrictions, WSJ reports
data.description:
• TikTok's Chinese parent, ByteDance (BDNCE) is assembling computing power with top Nvidia (NVDA) chips ‌outside China, the Wall Street Journal reported on Thursday.
  • ByteDance is working with Southeast Asian firm Aolani Cloud to ​deploy about 500 Nvidia Blackwell computing systems in Malaysia, totaling roughly 36,000 B200 chips, the Journal reported, citing people familiar with the matter.
  • Aolani acquires these servers from Aivres, ⁠a company that assembles servers using Nvidia chips, the report said, adding that ⁠if the plan goes through, the hardware involved could cost more than $2.5 billion.
  • An Aolani spokesman told WSJ ​the company ​was currently operating with ​about $100 million in hardware. ByteDance plans to use the computing power for AI research and development outside China and meet growing global demand for AI from its customers, as per the report.
  • The development comes as U.S. officials are said to be considering caps on the number of AI accelerators Nvidia Corp. (NVDA) can export to any one Chinese company, which would further constrain the chipmaker’s reentry into a crucial market. Officials at the US Commerce Department have also reportedly written draft regulations that would restrict AI chip shipments to anywhere in the world without American approval.

data.title: Meta delays rollout of new AI model ‘Avocado’ amid performance concerns, NYT reports
data.description:
Meta’s (META) new foundational A.I. model, which the company has been working on for months, has fallen short of the performance of leading A.I. models from rivals like Google (GOOG) (GOOGL), OpenAI (OPENAI) and Anthropic (ANTHRO) on internal tests for reasoning, coding and writing, the New York Times reported on Thursday, citing sources.

The model, code-named Avocado, outperformed Meta’s previous A.I. model and did better than Google’s Gemini 2.5 model from March, two of the people said. But it has not performed as strongly as Gemini 3.0 from November, they said.

As a result, Meta (META) has delayed Avocado’s release to at least May from this month, the people said. They added that the leaders of Meta’s A.I. division had instead discussed temporarily licensing Gemini to power the company’s A.I. products, though no decisions have been reached.

Meta, which owns Facebook, Instagram and Threads, has spent billions hiring top A.I. researchers and committed $600 billion to building data centers to power the technology. In January, Meta projected that it would spend as much as $135 billion this year, nearly twice the $72 billion it spent last year.

data.title: Afya Non-GAAP EPS of R$2.25, revenue of R$912.99M; gives FY26 outlook
data.description:
• Afya press release (AFYA): Q4 Non-GAAP EPS of R$2.25.
  • Revenue of R$912.99M (+7.5% Y/Y).
  • 4Q25 Adjusted EBITDA increased 6.1% YoY, reaching R$388.5 million, with an Adjusted EBITDA Margin of 42.6%. 
  • Operating Cash Conversion ratio of 93.7% and a Free Cash Flow record of R$1,056 million, with a solid cash position of R$ 1,125.4 million.
  • ~301 thousand users in Afya’s ecosystem.
  • "On the solid basis of our guidance achievement for 2025, we are now presenting our guidance for 2026. We expect Revenue to range between R$3,950 million and R$ 4,100 million, and Adjusted EBITDA to be between R$1,700 million and R$1,800 million, excluding any acquisition that may be concluded after the issuance of this guidance."

data.title: Abacus outlines path to $5B fee-paying AUM by 2026 while expanding recurring revenue mix
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Abacus Global Management, Inc. (ABX) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Jay Jackson stated, “Abacus closed the year by delivering another exceptional quarter, our 11th consecutive quarter of beating consensus. Today, I want to walk you through how we are executing against our vision and what the path forward looks like grounded, not in projections, but in what I'd call our proof point. A track record of consistent, measurable outperformance.”
  • Jackson emphasized that Abacus has tripled adjusted net income and adjusted EBITDA, expanded margins from 48% to 60%, and grown its asset base more than thirty-five-fold from under $100 million to nearly $3.6 billion over 11 quarters, with ROE and ROIC consistently at 20% or higher.
  • He announced full year 2026 outlook for adjusted net income of $96 million to $104 million, implying up to 22% year-over-year growth from 2025’s $85.7 million. Jackson also highlighted a newly authorized $20 million share repurchase program, adding to the previous $10 million buyback and ongoing dividends.
  • The CEO detailed the four vertical strategy: Life Solutions, Asset Group, Data/Technology (Abacus Intel), and Wealth Advisors, with the recent $50 million minority investment in Manning & Napier, a platform with over $18 billion AUM, strengthening the wealth advisory channel.
  • Jackson outlined long-term goals, including $450 million in EBITDA and 70% recurring revenue within five years, and $2.5 billion in revenue, $1.5 billion in EBITDA, and $150 billion in AUM by 2030.
  • CFO William McCauley stated, “Total revenue in the fourth quarter grew 116% to $71.9 million, compared to $33.2 million in the prior year period. Our growth was primarily driven by strong performance in Life Solutions, higher asset management fees and contributions from our technology services business.”

Outlook

  • Management initiated full year 2026 adjusted net income guidance of $96 million to $104 million, reflecting growth of up to 22% over 2025 results.
  • Abacus targets more than $5 billion in fee-paying AUM by the end of 2026, with a path to $50 billion by 2030. Jackson stated, “We are targeting over $3 million in technology revenue for 2026 with significant M&A upside as we expand into insurance, pension and mortgage verticals.”
  • The strategic focus remains on expanding recurring revenue from 16% of total revenue currently to 70% over the next five years.

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter revenue increased 116% to $71.9 million, driven by Life Solutions, asset management fees, and technology services.
  • Full year 2025 revenue rose 110% to $235.2 million.
  • Adjusted net income for Q4 2025 grew 71% to $23 million; full year adjusted net income rose 84% to $85.7 million.
  • Adjusted EBITDA for Q4 increased 132% to $38.6 million; full year adjusted EBITDA reached $132.6 million, up 115%.
  • Adjusted EBITDA margin was 54% for Q4 and 56% for the full year.
  • GAAP net income for Q4 was $7.2 million, compared to a net loss of $18.3 million in the prior year.
  • Balance sheet: $38.1 million in cash, $469.8 million in policy assets, $405.8 million in long-term debt as of year-end.

Q&A

  • Patrick Davitt, Autonomous Research: Asked about investor demand for securitizations and future size. Jackson replied, demand has increased and clarified, “The first one was $50 million. And as we look forward, whether that's $100 million or larger, those are some of the areas that we're targeting.”
  • Davitt followed with a question on equity raises. Jackson responded, “We don't have any intent to put out more equity to fund balance sheet purchases related to policy purchases.”
  • Crispin Love, Piper Sandler: Queried the capital deployed growth and margin dynamics. Jackson explained that gross inflows included over $100 million on the ETF side, which generally carries lower recurring revenue fees, and that excess cash from timing of deployment affected margin blending.
  • Love asked about achieving $450 million in adjusted EBITDA in five years. Jackson said the plan blends organic growth and acquisitions, with Manning & Napier as a key component.
  • Andrew Kligerman, TD Cowen: Asked about equity issuance by founders and acquisition pipeline. Jackson stated, “We still feel it's dramatically undervalued on a comparative basis. If there were to be a consideration related to some equity being sold by insiders, it would be in a controlled fashion.” He confirmed a robust and disciplined acquisition pipeline.
  • Kligerman questioned portfolio KPIs, specifically days held. Jackson noted strategic holding of some policies to maximize returns.
  • Timothy D'Agostino, B. Riley Securities: Asked about Abacus Intel data value. Jackson highlighted MVerify and LifeARC, stating, “We're able to verify when a mortality occurs in the United States within 48 hours with nearly 100% accuracy.”
  • D'Agostino asked about raising AUM guidance from $4 billion to $5 billion. Jackson said the increase is based on visible demand and new products.
  • Mike Grondahl, Northland Capital Markets: Asked about capital deployment for securitizations. Jackson confirmed the $50 million securitization was included in Q4 totals and said the balance sheet has excess capacity for future deals. Grondahl also asked about Manning & Napier’s impact on Wealth Advisors; Jackson said the investment complements and accelerates the strategy.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed management on recurring themes of capital deployment, equity issuance, and acquisition strategy, reflecting cautious optimism with persistent focus on growth sustainability and capital discipline.
  • Management maintained a confident and assertive tone, frequently referencing their track record, with Jackson stating, “We have conviction in our business model. We have confidence in our execution, and we have clarity on the path forward.”
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the tone remains confident, but with greater emphasis on disciplined expansion and recurring revenue, while analysts continue to probe for details on execution risks and capital strategy.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for adjusted net income increased from $80-84 million in Q3 to $96-104 million for 2026, signaling higher growth expectations.
  • Management’s language has evolved to focus more on fee-paying AUM, recurring revenue, and technology-enabled expansion, compared to greater emphasis on origination and one-time gains in the prior quarter.
  • Analysts’ focus shifted from initial dividend and buyback announcements last quarter to detailed questions about execution and scalability this quarter.
  • Key metrics such as capital deployed, AUM, and technology revenue targets have all moved upward, with a more explicit path to long-term recurring revenue.
  • Management’s confidence is now underpinned by 11 consecutive quarters of outperformance and expanded investor communication efforts.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted the need to educate the market to close the disconnect between fundamentals and valuation, noting, “The challenge is not performance, it is perception.”
  • Potential overreliance on acquisition integration and disciplined capital deployment as Abacus pursues rapid expansion across new verticals.
  • Analysts raised concerns about the sustainability of growth, the structure and timing of equity issuance, and execution risks in scaling wealth and technology platforms.

Final Takeaway

Abacus Global Management’s management emphasized disciplined execution and a clear strategy to scale recurring revenue to 70% of total revenue over five years, supported by robust capital deployment, expanding technology offerings, and a strengthened wealth advisory platform. With a significant increase in 2026 net income guidance, a new share repurchase program, and ongoing focus on high-margin, uncorrelated assets, the company aims to deliver both growth and shareholder returns while advancing toward its long-term vision of a fully integrated alternative asset management platform.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: El Pollo Loco targets 18–20 new restaurants in 2026 while advancing menu innovation
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: El Pollo Loco Holdings, Inc. (LOCO) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Elizabeth Williams highlighted "strong fourth quarter results that cap off a transformative second year" and noted "positive quarter of same-store sales growth, including stable traffic despite the ongoing macroeconomic challenges." Williams stated, "This top line momentum, combined with our team's relentless focus on operational excellence also enabled us to achieve better-than-expected restaurant level margins."
  • Williams detailed the success of new menu items: "Our Double Chicken Street Corn and Queso Crunch Burrito Bowls that we launched in late September...exceeding our expectations in both guest response and sales contribution." Both bowls are now permanent menu items. Additionally, she described a robust innovation pipeline with upcoming launches including Loco Tenders and new beverage concepts.
  • The company reported expanding its footprint to 9 states with 9 new restaurant openings in 2025, 6 of which were outside California and 7 utilizing second-generation sites to lower build costs. Williams shared, "The restaurants we've opened since 2024 are averaging over $2 million annually."
  • Williams announced the addition of a Chief Technology Officer and a new VP of Franchise Recruiting to accelerate franchise development and technology deployment.
  • CFO Ira Fils reported, "For the fourth quarter ended December 31, 2025, total revenue was $123.5 million compared to $114.3 million in the fourth quarter of 2024." Fils also noted, "Adjusted EBITDA for the fourth quarter of 2025 was $16.9 million compared to $14.3 million in the fourth quarter of 2024."

Outlook

  • Williams stated the company is "targeting approximately 18 to 20 new restaurant openings with 3 to 4 being company-owned locations" in 2026, with the majority outside California.
  • Fils provided 2026 guidance: "System-wide comparable store sales growth of 2% to 3%, the opening of 3 to 4 company-operated restaurants and 15 to 16 franchise-operated restaurants, capital spending between $37 million to $40 million; G&A expenses between $52 million to $54 million... Adjusted EBITDA between $66 million and $68 million."
  • For 2027 and 2028: "System-wide comparable restaurant growth percent in the low single digits, system-wide restaurant growth percent in the mid-single digits and adjusted EBITDA growth percent in the high single digits."

Financial Results

  • Company-operated restaurant revenue increased 7.1% to $102.4 million; franchise revenue increased 15.5% to $13 million.
  • The fourth quarter included 14 weeks compared to 13 weeks in the prior year, contributing $5.3 million in sales and $770,000 in adjusted EBITDA.
  • Restaurant contribution margin improved to 17.5% from 16.7% in the year-ago period.
  • GAAP net income was $6.5 million or $0.22 per diluted share; adjusted net income was $7.3 million or $0.25 per diluted share.
  • As of December 31, 2025, debt outstanding was $51 million and cash and cash equivalents were $6.2 million. Subsequent debt reduction brought the figure to $46 million as of March 12, 2026.

Q&A

  • Jake Bartlett, Truist Securities, asked about consumer demand and marketing complexity. Williams responded, "The consumer is still looking for great food at a great value... we're able to serve that for the consumer." On menu complexity, she explained, "Thoughtfully pacing and sequencing is really key... in many instances, as we're adding, we're also removing."
  • Todd Brooks, Benchmark Company, inquired about franchise growth mix and guidance confidence. Williams described a "healthy pipeline of existing franchise partners, but then there's also new franchise partners." On guidance, she cited "really terrific leadership team" and improved brand health, adding, "We've stabilized and improved... the margins and the profitability of the brand."
  • Jeremy Hamblin, Craig-Hallum, probed traffic differences between company and franchise restaurants and innovation cadence. Williams said, "I wouldn't read too much into it... it's usually a multitude of factors," and confirmed ongoing innovation: "We have a belief that the category loves innovation... our brand leans into that exploration."
  • Andrew Barish, Jefferies, asked about gas prices' impact and pricing strategy. Williams replied, "Surprisingly, we haven't" seen notable gas price impact. Fils said, "Our pricing will be similar to that as we move forward into '26."
  • Tania Anderson, William Blair, asked about new store opening cadence and COGS initiatives. Fils noted, "We have 8 stores under construction right now... focus for 2026, it's taking things and having the supplier do some of the prep for us."
  • Matthew Curtis, D.A. Davidson, asked about new market performance. Williams stated, "In Kent, Washington... well, well, well above our system average... New Mexico, also... performing really well, above average."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' sentiment was generally positive, with frequent congratulations on results and momentum, but questions focused on competitive positioning, menu innovation, and new unit growth indicated a keen interest in sustainability and strategic execution.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone in both prepared remarks and exchanges, with Williams and Fils emphasizing operational discipline, innovation, and margin improvement. Williams remarked, "We have tremendous confidence in our ability to accelerate growth over the next several years."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the tone from both management and analysts remains optimistic but now features increased confidence due to delivered results and the introduction of multi-year guidance.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter introduced multi-year guidance for the first time, reflecting increased confidence and strategic clarity.
  • Sales growth and margin improvement outpaced the prior quarter, with higher revenue and improved restaurant contribution margin.
  • Discussion shifted more toward innovation pipeline execution, digital engagement, and franchise expansion outside California.
  • Management's tone is more assertive regarding future growth, while analysts have shifted from probing recovery to focusing on scalability and long-term strategy.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited macroeconomic challenges and noted "the consumer is certainly still looking... for just a great experience at a great value."
  • Fils highlighted planned increases in G&A and capital spending to support growth and technology initiatives.
  • Analysts raised questions about menu complexity, franchise pipeline robustness, and the sustainability of innovation and traffic gains.
  • No significant impact from gas prices or adverse consumer demand trends was observed in the current quarter.

Final Takeaway

El Pollo Loco's management emphasized that 2025 marked a turning point with sustained sales growth, margin expansion, and strategic investments in menu innovation, digital engagement, and national expansion. With a robust pipeline of new products, disciplined operational execution, and the largest new unit development plan since 2022, the company signals confidence in accelerating its growth trajectory through 2026 and beyond.
